title: Spinning Cheviot wool
subtitle: Preparing and spinning the springy, resilient fleece of the Cheviot breed into a hardwearing two-ply
excerpt: Cheviot is a medium-coarse British upland breed with a bouncy, resilient staple that resists compression and produces a yarn with good memory. This entry covers washing, carding, and spinning a balanced two-ply suited to socks, outerwear, and hardwearing accessories.
sourceNotes: Cheviot fleece preparation and spinning synthesised from general British breed spinning references. The Cheviot is a hill breed native to the Cheviot Hills on the Anglo-Scottish border.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of an individual fleece lock from cut end to tip, measured after washing. Cheviot typically has a staple of 7-12 cm.
The natural waviness along the length of a wool fibre. Cheviot has a pronounced, open crimp that gives the finished yarn its springy, resilient character.
A spinning method in which fibres are drafted loosely from a carded preparation, creating a lofty, springy yarn with air trapped between the fibres.
A yarn produced by plying two singles together in the direction opposite to the spinning direction, giving a balanced, round yarn.

content.text: Cheviot fleece is one of the most satisfying British breeds to spin. The strong

gives the singles a springiness that is easy to feel as you draft, and the finished yarn has a resilience that suits hardwearing items. The fleece is not soft enough for next-to-skin wear but is excellent for socks, bags, hats, and outer garments. The
of 7-12 cm gives comfortable drafting on hand cards or a drum carder.

items.name: Raw Cheviot fleece, skirted and washed

content.text: Cheviot fleece is moderately greasy and washes well. Soak the skirted fleece in hot water (55-60°C) with washing-up liquid for 20 minutes without agitation. Drain, rinse twice at the same temperature, and spread on a mesh rack to dry. The washed fleece will be an off-white or pale cream; Cheviot is not as bright as some fine breeds but has a pleasant lustre when clean.

Load the hand card in the direction of the staple and card in short strokes, transferring the fibre 3-4 times to open the locks and remove any remaining vegetable matter. Roll off as a firm rolag. Cheviot cards readily: the open crimp means the fibres do not mat together as easily as fine breeds. Sort out any second cuts (short fragments left from shearing) during carding: they do not draft smoothly and create lumps in the singles.
Set the wheel to a moderate to high twist ratio. Use the

: draw the rolag back from the orifice with the back hand while the front hand controls the flow of fibre. Allow twist to run slightly into the drafting zone before drawing back. The finished singles should feel lively and springy: Cheviot singles have considerably more bounce than merino or fine-staple breeds at the same diameter. Aim for a DK to aran weight single depending on the intended end use.
Step 4: Ply and set the twist
Wind two full bobbins and ply in the S-direction at the same wheel setting used for spinning. The

should hang straight when a cut length is held mid-air. Wind off onto the niddy-noddy and tie the skein loosely in four places. Soak in warm water for 20 minutes, squeeze gently, snap the skein firmly between both hands twice to set the ply, and hang to dry under light tension. The finished yarn will have a firm, round structure and a pronounced spring when squeezed.

items.problem: The singles have frequent lumps at regular intervals
items.cause: Second cuts from shearing were not removed during carding
items.fix: Pick over each carded rolag before spinning and remove short fibre fragments. Work through the carding more slowly, transferring fibre an extra time.

items.problem: The plied yarn is lively and coils back on itself when tension is released
items.cause: The singles twist was too high relative to the ply twist, leaving residual energy in the yarn
items.fix: Add more ply twist to balance the singles twist, or soak the finished skein and snap it more firmly before hanging. A second soak sometimes settles the twist.

items.problem: The finished yarn feels rougher than expected for Cheviot
items.cause: The fleece may be from a ram or from an older animal, both of which produce coarser fibre
items.fix: Cheviot is not a soft breed. Reserve it for socks, bags, hats, and outerwear rather than garments worn next to skin. If softness is needed, blend with 20% merino before carding.
